instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Must Know Income Tax Penalties in India
The Indian tax assessment and collection system is designed to create a streamlined process to ensure that there are no loopholes. However, due to honest mistakes or fraudulent intentions, taxpayers may fail to pay their taxes on time. In the context of the Income Tax Act of 1961, any taxpayer who defaults on tax liabilities is subjected to certain penalties. Income Tax penalties vary depending on the default type, and as a taxpayer, you must be aware of these penalties.
Tax penalties that every taxpayer must know
The list of income tax penalties under the Income Tax Act is quite long, so let’s take a look at some of the common income tax fines that you must be aware of:
Failure to report income
One of the most common income tax penalties is imposed when taxpayers fail to report their income correctly while filling tax returns. Under Section 270A of the Income Tax Act, under-reporting attracts a penalty equal to 50% of the tax payable. However, if misreporting occurs, such as hiding income, furnishing false details or suppressing facts, the penalty can increase to 200% of the tax due. These stringent income tax penalties underscore the importance of accurate disclosure and timely filling of returns.
Non-maintenance of account books
The Income Tax Act mandates the maintenance of specific books of accounts for tax purposes, as outlined under Section 44AA. However, there is a penalty for not maintaining, keeping, or retaining books of account and documents as required under section 44AA. This penalty of Rs 25,0001 is levied under section 271A.
Default in paying tax liabilities
The Budget 2025  has proposed zero tax liability on income up to ₹12,00,000 for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) under the new tax regime .* Here’s how:The updated income tax slabs  for the new regime in FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) are:
Under the revised tax structure, individuals earning up to ₹12,00,000 will have zero tax liability due to an increased rebate of ₹60,000. For salaried individuals, income up to ₹12,75,000 will be tax-free, factoring in the ₹75,000 standard deduction.
Marginal relief on rebate remains applicable.
The rebate does not apply to income taxed at special rates, such as capital gains under Section 112A.
Note:
Know that a default in paying tax liabilities according to the tax slab subjects the taxpayer to penalties as decided by the assessing officer.   Under section 221 (1) of the Income Tax Act, the tax penalty amount cannot be more than the tax arrears on the taxpayer.
Failure or delay to file TDS/TCS return
Sections 200(3), 206C(3) and 234E of the Income Tax Act deal with the provisions related to TDS and TCS returns. The default in filing TDS or TCS returns subjects the taxpayer to a penalty of Rs. 200 per day until the return is made. Know that the penalty cannot exceed the TDS or TCS amount.
Failure to comply with the Income-tax notice
Under Section 272A(1), failure to comply with income-tax notices, such as ignoring summons, refusing to answer questions, or not signing statements, can attract an income tax fine of ₹10,000 per default, with an opportunity to be heard before imposition.
Key Dates to Remember
The following are some of the essential dates that mark the timeline for earning, assessing and settling your tax obligations under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Financial Year
The Financial Year (FY), also known as the previous year for income tax purposes, is the specific, continuous 12-month period during which an individual's income is accrued or earned. This universally accepted accounting period in India begins on April 1 and concludes precisely on March 31 of the immediately subsequent calendar year. All forms of earnings generated within this defined timeframe, whether from salary, business, capital gains, or other sources, are grouped to form the total income that will be subject to assessment and taxation in the following year.
Assessment Year
The Assessment Year (AY) is the 12 months commencing on April 1st immediately after the close of the Financial Year. During this year, the income earned in the previous Financial Year is assessed and taxed by the Income Tax Department. Failing to file your return within the Assessment Year’s deadline can result in an income tax late fee and other financial consequences.
End of Financial Year
March 31 marks the legal end of the Financial Year and serves as a hard cutoff for numerous tax planning activities. To validly claim deductions for various tax-saving investments, such as those made under Section 80C* (for avenues like life insurance, Public Provident Fund, or Equity Linked Savings Scheme) or Section 80D* (for health insurance premiums), you must make the payments and keep the documentation for these investments on or before this precise date.
*These deductions/benefits are available only under the old tax regime.
Income Tax Return Filing Deadline
The deadline for submitting the Income Tax Return for individual taxpayers who are not subject to an audit is July 31 of the relevant Assessment Year as per Section 139(1). You must strictly follow this date, as any ITR filed after this due date is classified as a ‘belated return’ under Section 139(4) and immediately attracts a mandatory income tax late fee under Section 234F. Additionally, interest applicable under Section 234A will also be charged.
Advance Tax Payment Deadlines
Advance tax is paying a portion of your estimated tax liability during the Financial Year itself. It applies to those whose tax liability exceeds ₹10,000. Non-presumptive taxpayers have to pay a percentage of their estimated tax liability in installments with due dates on June 15, September 15, December 15 and March 15. For presumptive taxpayers under Section 44AD, 44ADA or 44AE, the entire advance tax liability is payable by March 15 of the financial year. Failure to pay or short payment by these dates may attract penal interest under Section 234C.
Belated/Revised Return Filling Deadline
If you miss the original deadline under Section 139(1), you can still file a belated return under Section 139(4). Similarly, if you discover an error in your original, timely-filed return, you can file a revised return under Section 139(5). The common deadline for filling both a belated or a revised return is December 31 of the relevant Assessment Year, or before the completion of the assessment, whichever is earlier.
Paying income tax liabilities: What you need to know
As a taxpayer, you must know that the due date for filing income tax returns is usually 31st July for non-audited taxpayers without late fees, unless the government has announced an exception or extension, and this FY 2024-25 (AY2025-26) is 31st July 20257. In case of default in paying tax, you are subject to the payment of the ITR fine and the due taxes.
Income tax penalties can be paid either through online or offline methods:
Online method:
To pay an income tax fine online, visit the official income tax e-filing portal and select “Self-Assessment Tax (300)” under Challan 280.  Payments can be made using NetBanking, debit/credit cards, or UPI through authorized banks and payment gateways. Always save the payment challan for your records.
Offline method:
To pay income tax penalties, you can visit the nearest authorized bank and fill out the form ITNS-2808 with relevant details. Submit the form along with the penalty amount either through RTGS or NEFT, and make sure to collect a receipt.4
Exploring legal provisions and reliefs
The Income Tax Act of 1961 ensures that every taxpayer is subjected to equity provisions, and defaulters are subjected to penalties. However, the government of India steps in to provide relief to taxpayers who may have missed the deadline inadvertently. For instance, the last date to file income tax returns was extended to 31st December 2024, thus providing a 6-month extension period to taxpayers. Remember that even if the government offers an extension, a delay in filing income tax returns can subject you to penalties ranging from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000.
Key Takeaways
Missing deadlines for filling or paying taxes can trigger income tax penalties. You can mark July 31 of the assessment year as the deadline to file your ITR.
Maintain accurate records and account books since failure to do so can result in a penalty of up to ₹25,000 under Section 271A.
As per Section 270A, a 50% tax penalty applies for under-reported income, which can increase to 200% if the under-reporting is due to misreporting.
Conclusion
Awareness of income tax penalties is essential to protect your finances. By filling returns on time, paying taxes correctly (according to your slab), and maintaining proper documentation, you can avoid hefty fines. Furthermore, proactive planning, keeping track of deadlines, and responding promptly to notices may reduce the risk of penalties.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What is the maximum penalty for not paying taxes?
The maximum penalty for defaulting on tax payments is usually limited to the tax arrears determined by the assessing officer under Section 221 of the Income Tax Act.
How to avoid tax penalties?
To avoid tax-related penalties, file accurate returns on time, maintain proper account books, pay due taxes promptly and respond to any notices from the Income Tax Department.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

output.final_text:
Must Know Income Tax Penalties in India
The Indian tax assessment and collection system is designed to create a streamlined process to ensure
that there are no loopholes. However, due to honest mistakes or fraudulent intentions, taxpayers may fail
to pay their taxes on time. In the context of the Income Tax Act of 1961, any taxpayer who defaults on tax
liabilities is subjected to certain penalties. Income Tax penalties vary depending on the default type, and
as a taxpayer, you must be aware of these penalties.
Tax penalties that every taxpayer must know
The list of income tax penalties under the Income Tax Act is quite long, so let’s take a look at some of the
common income tax fines that you must be aware of:
1. Failure to report income
One of the most common income tax penalties is imposed when taxpayers fail to report their
income correctly while filling tax returns. Under Section 270A of the Income Tax Act, under-
reporting attracts a penalty equal to 50% of the tax payable. However, if misreporting occurs,
such as hiding income, furnishing false details, or suppressing facts, the penalty can increase to
200% of the tax due. These stringent income tax penalties underscore the importance of
accurate disclosure and timely filling of returns.
2. Non-maintenance of account books
The Income Tax Act mandates the maintenance of specific books of accounts for tax purposes,
as outlined under Section 44AA. However, there is a penalty for not maintaining, keeping, or
retaining books of accounts and documents as required under section 44AA. This penalty of Rs
25,0001 is levied under section 271A.
3. Default in paying tax liabilities
The Budget 2025 has proposed zero tax liability on income up to ₹12,00,000 for FY 2025-26 (AY
2026-27) under the new tax regime .* Here’s how:
The updated income tax slabs for the new regime in FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27) are:
Income Earned in a Financial Year Tax Liabilities
Up to Rs. 4,00,000 NIL
Rs. 4,00,001- Rs. 8,00,000 5%
Rs. 8,00,001- Rs. 12,00,000 10%
Rs. 12,00,001- Rs. 16,00,000 15%
Rs. 16,00,001- Rs. 20,00,000 20%
Rs. 20,00,001- Rs. 24,00,000 25%
Rs. 24,00,001 and Above 30%
Under the revised tax structure, individuals earning up to ₹12,00,000 will have zero tax liability due to an
increased rebate of ₹60,000. For salaried individuals, income up to ₹12,75,000 will be tax-free, factoring
in the ₹75,000 standard deduction.
a. Marginal relief on rebate remains applicable.
b. The rebate does not apply to income taxed at special rates, such as capital gains under
Section 112A.
Note:
Know that a default in paying tax liabilities according to the tax slab subjects the taxpayer to penalties as
decided by the assessing officer. Under section 221 (1) of the Income Tax Act, the tax penalty amount
cannot be more than the tax arrears on the taxpayer.
4. Failure or delay to file TDS/TCS return
Sections 200(3), 206C(3) and 234E of the Income Tax Act deal with the provisions related to TDS
and TCS returns. The default in filing TDS or TCS returns subjects the taxpayer to a penalty of Rs.
200 per day until the return is made. Know that the penalty cannot exceed the TDS or TCS
amount.
5. Failure to comply with the Income-tax notice
Under Section 272A(1) of the Income Tax Act, failure to comply with income-tax notices, such as
ignoring summons, refusing to answer questions, or not signing statements made by them in the
course of any proceedings under the Income Tax Act , can attract an income tax fine of ₹10,000
per default, with an opportunity to be heard before imposition.
Key Dates to Remember
The following are some of the essential dates that mark the timeline for earning, assessing and settling
your tax obligations under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Financial Year
The Financial Year (FY), also known as the previous year for income tax purposes, is the specific,
continuous 12-month period during which an individual's income is accrued or earned. This universally
accepted accounting period in India begins on April 1 and concludes precisely on March 31 of the
immediately subsequent calendar year. All forms of earnings generated within this defined timeframe,
whether from salary, business, capital gains, or other sources, are grouped to form the total income that
will be subject to assessment and taxation in the following year.
Assessment Year
The Assessment Year (AY) is the 12 months commencing on April 1st immediately after the close of the
Financial Year. During this year, the income earned in the previous Financial Year is assessed and taxed
by the Income Tax Department. Failing to file your return within the Assessment Year’s deadline can
result in an income tax late fee and other financial consequences.
End of Financial Year
March 31 marks the end of the Financial Year and serves as a hard cutoff for numerous tax planning
activities. To validly claim deductions for various tax-saving investments, such as those made under
Section 80C* (for avenues like life insurance, Public Provident Fund, or Equity Linked Savings Scheme) or
Section 80D* (for health insurance premiums), you must make the payments and keep the
documentation for these investments on or before this precise date.
*These deductions/benefits are available only under the old tax regime.
Income Tax Return Filing Deadline
The deadline for submitting the Income Tax Return for individual taxpayers who are not subject to an
audit is July 31 of the relevant Assessment Year as per Section 139(1). You must strictly follow this date,
as any ITR filed after this due date is classified as a ‘belated return’ under Section 139(4).
Advance Tax Payment Deadlines
Advance tax is paying a portion of your estimated tax liability during the Financial Year itself. It applies to
those whose tax liability exceeds ₹10,000. Non-presumptive taxpayers have to pay a percentage of their
estimated tax liability in installments with due dates on June 15, September 15, December 15 and March
15. For presumptive taxpayers under Section 44AD, 44ADA or 44AE, the entire advance tax liability is
payable by March 15 of the financial year. Failure to pay or short payment by these dates may attract
penal interest under Section 234C.
Belated/Revised Return Filling Deadline
If you miss the original deadline under Section 139(1), you can still file a belated return under Section
139(4). Similarly, if you discover an error in your original, timely-filed return, you can file a revised return
under Section 139(5). The common deadline for filling both a belated or a revised return is December 31
of the relevant Assessment Year, or before the completion of the assessment, whichever is earlier.
Paying income tax liabilities: What you need to know
As a taxpayer, you must know that the due date for filing income tax returns is usually 31st July for non-
audited taxpayers without late fees, unless the government has announced an exception or extension,
and this FY 2024-25 (AY2025-26) is 31st July 20257. In case of default in paying tax, you are subject to the
payment of the ITR fine and the due taxes.
Income tax penalties can be paid either through online or offline methods:
1. Online method:
To pay an income tax fine online, visit the official income tax e-filing portal and select “Self- Assessment Tax (300)” under Challan 280. Payments can be made using Net Banking, debit/credit cards, or UPI through authorized banks and payment gateways. Always save the payment challan for your records.
2. Offline method:
To pay income tax penalties, you can visit the nearest authorized bank and fill out the form
ITNS-2808 with relevant details. Submit the form along with the penalty amount either through
RTGS or NEFT, and make sure to collect a receipt.4
Exploring legal provisions and reliefs
The Income Tax Act of 1961 ensures that every taxpayer is subjected to equity provisions, and defaulters
are subjected to penalties. However, the government of India steps in to provide relief to taxpayers who
may have missed the deadline inadvertently. For instance, the last date to file income tax returns was
extended to 31st December 2024, thus providing a 6-month extension period to taxpayers. Remember
that even if the government offers an extension, a delay in filing income tax returns can subject you to
penalties ranging from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 5,000.
Key Takeaways
● Missing deadlines for filling or paying taxes can lead to income tax penalties. You can mark July 31 of the assessment year as the deadline to file your ITR.
● Maintain accurate records and account books since failure to do so can result in a penalty of up to ₹25,000 under Section 271A.1
● As per Section 270A, a 50% tax penalty applies for under-reported income, which can increase to 200% if the under-reporting is due to misreporting.2
Conclusion
Awareness of income tax penalties is essential to protect your finances. By filling returns on time, paying
taxes correctly (according to your slab), and maintaining proper documentation, you can avoid hefty fines.
Furthermore, proactive planning, keeping track of deadlines, and responding promptly to notices may
reduce the risk of penalties.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What is the maximum penalty for not paying taxes?
The maximum penalty for defaulting on tax payments is usually limited to the tax arrears determined by the assessing officer under Section 221 of the Income Tax Act.
How to avoid tax penalties?
To avoid tax-related penalties, file accurate returns on time, maintain proper account books, pay due taxes promptly and respond to any notices from the Income Tax Department.

metadata.title: Revamp-Must Know Income Tax Penalties in India
